title: The School Fee Dilemma
narrative: Your daughter Priya's school fees of ₹3,000 are due next week. You have ₹4,500 in your Household jar, but the landlord is also asking for this month's rent of ₹2,500. What will you do?

choices.text: Pay school fees first, negotiate rent delay

impact.feedback: Smart choice! Education is an investment. Talking to your landlord showed great communication skills.

choices.text: Pay rent first, ask school for extension

impact.feedback: Rent is important, but delaying education fees can add late charges. Consider prioritizing long-term goals.

choices.text: Borrow from a neighbor to pay both

impact.feedback: Borrowing creates a new debt. It's better to negotiate directly than to add more financial obligations.

title: The Festival Temptation
narrative: Diwali is around the corner! Your SHG friends are all buying new clothes and sweets. You have ₹2,000 in your Children jar and ₹5,000 in Savings. Your neighbor suggests pooling money for bulk shopping to save 30%.

choices.text: Use only ₹1,500 from Children jar, join bulk shopping

impact.feedback: Brilliant! You celebrated Diwali AND saved money through smart bulk buying. Your savings stay protected!

choices.text: Spend all ₹2,000 from Children jar on individual shopping

impact.feedback: You enjoyed the festival but spent more than needed. Bulk buying with friends could have saved you ₹600!

choices.text: Dip into Savings for a grand celebration

impact.feedback: Raiding savings for wants is risky. What if an emergency strikes next month without your safety net?

title: Medical Emergency
narrative: Your mother-in-law suddenly falls ill and needs ₹4,000 for hospital bills. You have ₹3,000 in your Emergency jar and ₹5,000 in Savings. Time is critical!

impact.feedback: This is exactly what the Emergency jar is for! Using minimal savings shows great financial discipline.

choices.text: Take a loan from moneylender at 5% monthly interest

impact.feedback: High-interest loans can trap you in a debt cycle. You had emergency funds available — always check your jars first!

choices.text: Use all Savings to pay and keep Emergency intact

impact.feedback: You protected the Emergency jar but drained Savings. The Emergency jar exists for moments like this!

title: The Micro-Business Opportunity
narrative: A wholesale dealer offers you 50 sarees at ₹200 each to sell at ₹400 in your village. You need ₹10,000 upfront. You have ₹8,000 in Savings. Your SHG can lend ₹5,000 at 1% monthly interest.

choices.text: Borrow ₹5,000 from SHG, use ₹5,000 from Savings

impact.feedback: Smart move! SHG loans have low interest, and you kept ₹3,000 in savings as backup. The potential profit of ₹10,000 far outweighs the small interest.

choices.text: Use all ₹8,000 from Savings, borrow ₹2,000 from neighbor

impact.feedback: Emptying savings is risky. If the sarees don't sell quickly, you have no safety net. SHG loans are safer than personal debt.

choices.text: Skip the opportunity — it's too risky

impact.feedback: Being cautious is okay, but calculated risks with SHG support can build wealth. ₹10,000 potential profit with SHG backing is reasonable!

title: The Insurance Decision
narrative: An insurance agent visits your SHG meeting and offers health insurance at ₹150/month per family member. Your family of 4 would cost ₹600/month. Last year, you spent ₹8,000 on medical bills.

impact.feedback: Starting small is practical! Cover the most vulnerable family members first. You can add more as income grows.
Full coverage is great, but ₹600/month is 4% of income. Make sure other jars aren't starved.

choices.text: Skip insurance — you've been healthy so far

impact.feedback: Last year's ₹8,000 medical bill was a warning. Insurance at ₹3,600/year vs ₹8,000 in bills — the math is clear!
